text.tspan: IDENTITY ADJUNCTS: three orthogonal pieces of the modern identity puzzle.
SCIM = user lifecycle. FIDO2/Passkeys = phishing-resistant auth.
Kerberos = enterprise SSO inside the perimeter.

g.text: SCIM (System for Cross-domain Identity Management) u2014 RFC 7644
FIDO2 / WEBAUTHN / PASSKEYS u2014 W3C + FIDO Alliance

text.tspan: MODERN ENTERPRISE STACK
u2022 Workday u2192 SCIM u2192 IdP u2192 SCIM u2192 SaaS apps (lifecycle)
u2022 IdP login uses PASSKEYS (or hardware key) (phishing-resistant)
u2022 IdP issues OIDC tokens for SaaS apps (web SSO)
u2022 IdP issues Kerberos tickets for AD-joined resources (LAN SSO)
All four work together; none replaces the others.
Problem: 50 SaaS apps u00d7 1000 employees u00d7 onboard/offboard/role-change
= manual chaos + ex-employees with active accounts (security risk)
FLOW (provisioning)
HR system (Workday/SAP) hires/fires user
IdP (Okta/Azure AD/Google) updates user store
u2193 SCIM
IdP pushes to every SaaS app via SCIM REST API:
POST /Users {userName, name, email, groups, role}
PATCH /Users/{id} {active: false} u2190 deprovision
PUT /Groups/{id}/members ...
SaaS apps (Slack/Zoom/GitHub/Salesforce/...) update their user lists
OUTCOME
- day 1: new hire has all accounts before they sit down
- day N: termination removes ALL access in minutes
- role change: permissions update across all apps
- audit: one place to query 'who has access to what'
Problem: passwords = phishing-vulnerable, leaked, reused, shared.
SMS 2FA = SIM-swappable. TOTP = phishable (real-time relay).
Need: auth that CANNOT be phished even if user is tricked.
FLOW (registration)
user creates passkey for 'github.com'
authenticator (phone/Yubikey/TPM/Touch ID) generates KEY PAIR
PUBLIC KEY u2192 sent to github.com (stored on user record)
PRIVATE KEY u2192 STAYS ON DEVICE, NEVER LEAVES
FLOW (login)
github.com sends a CHALLENGE bound to 'github.com'
authenticator signs ONLY IF site origin == 'github.com'
user authorizes with biometric / PIN (local; no secret transmitted)
signed challenge u2192 github.com
github.com verifies signature with stored public key
OUTCOME
- phishing-resistant: signature is bound to origin; attacker site can't get one
- no shared secret to leak in a breach
- device-bound: stealing the password file doesn't help
- SYNCED PASSKEYS (Apple/Google/MS): roam across user's devices via cloud sync (a usability trade-off)
Problem: inside an enterprise, every user uses many services every day.
Don't want to type password 50x. Don't want password sent to each service.
Need: prove identity once; get short-lived tickets for each service.
FLOW (simplified)
1. user logs in u2192 AS (Authentication Server) issues TGT (Ticket-Granting Ticket)
TGT encrypted with user's key; user decrypts; proves identity
2. user wants to access fileserver u2192 asks TGS (Ticket-Granting Server) for a service ticket
TGS issues service ticket encrypted with fileserver's key
3. user presents service ticket to fileserver u2192 access granted
fileserver never sees password; never talks to AS
KDC = AS + TGS = Key Distribution Center
OUTCOME
- SSO inside the realm (one login)
- mutual auth (services prove identity to user too)
- foundation of Active Directory authentication
- works offline-from-internet (great for corporate LAN)
- LIMITATIONS: time sync critical (clock skew breaks tickets);
cross-realm trust complex; not internet-scale (use OIDC for that)
